issueTitle: tools: P2P traffic contract testing and pattern matching

issue.title: tools: P2P traffic contract testing and pattern matching
issue.author: 0xB10C
issue.description:
@janb84 recelenty suggested to me that it might be interesting to do "contract testing" of the P2P messages send between our peer-observer node and our peers. Contract testing is "a software testing technique that validates interactions between service providers and consumers, ensuring they adhere to a shared "contract" (API structure)" - for us that's the P2P protocol. 

There are at least two ways to go about this:

1. We define a "contract" based on Bitcoin P2P protocol knowledge (e.g. an outbound ping should be followed by an inbound ping from the same peer). This is a technique used in API testing.
2. We collect a bit of P2P traffic between a peer-observer node and a known Bitcoin Core node and extract a "pattern" from it. This is something common in network firewalls.

Then, we check real-time traffic and also archived traffic (#121) in a tool against the "contract" or "patter". One out-come of this could be: "This node claims to be /Satoshi:31.0.0/", but it's traffic with us doesn't at all look like a Bitcoin Core node (i.e. fake user_agent). What's going on here?".

Opening this for discussion - I think there are no concrete tasks to implement right now.
requirements: We define a "contract" based on Bitcoin P2P protocol knowledge (e.g. an outbound ping should be followed by an inbound ping from the same peer). This is a technique used in API testing.
We collect a bit of P2P traffic between a peer-observer node and a known Bitcoin Core node and extract a "pattern" from it. This is something common in network firewalls.
should be followed by an inbound ping from the same peer)

resources.title: README Context
resources.content:
# peer-observer

Tool to monitor for P2P anomalies and attacks using well-behaving, passive
Bitcoin Core honeynodes (honeypot nodes).

## Components and their interaction

The peer-observer consists of multiple components. One or more `extractors` that
extract events from a Bitcoin Core node and multiple `tools` that process the
extracted data. The `extractors` and `tools` are connected with a [nats.io]-based
PUB-SUB connection via a NATS server. The exchanged messages are serialized
protobuf structures.

The `ebpf-extractor` uses the Bitcoin Core tracepoints to extract
events like received and send P2P messages, open and closed P2P connections, mempool
changes, and more. This is implemented using the USDT capabilites of [libbpf-rs].
The Bitcoin P2P protocol messages are deserialized using [rust-bitcoin].

The `rpc-extractor` periodically queries the Bitcoin Core RPC interface using
[corepc]. Results are published to NATS as RPC events.

The `p2p-extractor` receives an inbound connection from a Bitcoin node and publishes
selected P2P measurements as events into a NATS pub-sub queue.

The `log-extractor` publishes them parsed `debug.log` log messages as events to NATS.

The tools are written in Rust (or any other language that supports NATS 
and protobuf). They subscribe to the NATS server. For example, the `logger` tool 
simply prints out all messages that it receives, the `metrics` tool produces prometheus 
metrics, and the `connectivity-check` tool tests received addresses if they are reachable.

pitfalls: Do not let transport code or persistence concerns leak into the rule engine.

resources.title: Current Code: tools/connectivity-check/README.md
resources.content:
# `connectivity-check` tool

> connects to IP addresses received via `addr(v2)` messages and records the result

A peer-observer tool that checks the connectivity of received addr(v2) message entries
by connecting to the addresses and trying to do a `version` handshake. The results are
recorded in a CSV file. Additionally, the tool offers prometheus metrics about the
connection results.

## Example

For example, connect to a NATS server on 128.0.0.1:1234 and start the metrics server on 127.0.0.1:8220:

```
$ cargo run --bin connectivity-check -- --nats-address 127.0.0.1:1234 --metrics-address 127.0.0.1:8220

or

$ ./target/[release/debug]/connectivity-check -n 127.0.0.1:1234 --metrics-address 127.0.0.1:8220
```

This then outputs something similar to:

```
$ curl 127.0.0.1:8220/metrics
...
# HELP connectivitycheck_addr_successful_connection_tor_exit Number of successful connections to addresses that we received from a Tor exit node.
# TYPE connectivitycheck_addr_successful_connection_tor_exit counter
connectivitycheck_addr_successful_connection_tor_exit{addr_version="addrv2",network="IPv4"} 10
# HELP connectivitycheck_addr_tried Number of addresses tried.
 ```

pitfalls: Do not over-document speculative future work as if it is already implemented.

resources.title: Current Code: README.md
resources.content:
# peer-observer

Tool to monitor for P2P anomalies and attacks using well-behaving, passive
Bitcoin Core honeynodes (honeypot nodes).

## Components and their interaction

The peer-observer consists of multiple components. One or more `extractors` that
extract events from a Bitcoin Core node and multiple `tools` that process the
extracted data. The `extractors` and `tools` are connected with a [nats.io]-based
PUB-SUB connection via a NATS server. The exchanged messages are serialized
protobuf structures.

The `ebpf-extractor` uses the Bitcoin Core tracepoints to extract
events like received and send P2P messages, open and closed P2P connections, mempool
changes, and more. This is implemented using the USDT capabilites of [libbpf-rs].
The Bitcoin P2P protocol messages are deserialized using [rust-bitcoin].

The `rpc-extractor` periodically queries the Bitcoin Core RPC interface using
[corepc]. Results are published to NATS as RPC events.

The `p2p-extractor` receives an inbound connection from a Bitcoin node and publishes
selected P2P measurements as events into a NATS pub-sub queue.

The `log-extractor` publishes them parsed `debug.log` log messages as events to NATS.

The tools are written in Rust (or any other language that supports NATS 
and protobuf). They subscribe to the NATS server. For example, the `logger` tool 
simply prints out all messages that it receives, the `metrics` tool produces prometheus 
metrics, and the `connectivity-check` tool tests received addresses if they are reachable.
